text: Thank you, John. Many of you in the audience think that surgical knowledge is an oxymoron. I'm going to actually try to actually give you some ideas of what we do in the operating room besides playing music and having fun. We can start the video.

So what I'm going to discuss today is basically an ileal pouch, and I particularly talk about three-stage procedures versus two-stage procedures, and what exactly are those. Let's just start that off right off the bat. This is a schematic. If you can just stop the video for a second.

On the left-hand side, this is actually the initial phase of a three-stage procedure, where actually you take just the colon out, but you leave the bottom-most portion, the rectum, in place. Is this working? No. Pardon me? Oh, the mouse is working. Okay. I don't think it's working. Okay. That's the initial stage. And then after, that's the first of three stages. The second of the three stages is actually when you take the rectum out and make an ileal J pouch showed on the right. And then the third is actually when there's an ileostomy still created and that ileostomy itself is reversed. We call that a three-stage procedure in our field.

On the right-hand side is where the Initial two phases are put together. The total abdominal colectomy and the ileal pouch is connected. The main difference being that the rectum is removed and the pouch is created. And in the final phase of that two-stage procedure, the ileostomy is reversed.
The surgeon marks a right angle triangle on the patient's abdomen to plan a Pfannenstiel incision, with a blue dot indicating the proposed ileostomy site at the upper right of the visual field.

The surgeon makes an incision below the hairline, through the various layers including Scarpus fascia and the anterior sheath of the rectus, revealing the muscle underneath. The incision measures approximately eight centimeters.

The surgeon opens the incision to access the abdominal cavity, preparing for hand-assisted ileal pouch analastomosis. A trocar is placed in the umbilicus, followed by another in the right lower quadrant, which will serve as the future ileostomy site. A gel port is used to insufflate the abdominal cavity with air, facilitating access.

The surgeon begins dissection of the right colon using a ligature device, which is visible entering the surgical field. This instrument is used to fuse tissue and achieve hemostasis during the procedure.

The surgeon mobilizes the colon at the hepatic flexure, with the gallbladder and liver visible in the background. Care is taken to avoid the kidney, ureter, and duodenum, which is shown beneath the colon. The colon is mobilized to the mid-transverse colon, with attention given to preserving the ileocolic artery for pouch length.
The surgeon identifies the ileocolic artery and the mesentery of the colon distal to it. An incision is made in the mesentery to gain access, and the process of mobilizing the colon begins.
The surgeon dissects the mesentery of the colon at the hepatic flexure, carefully avoiding injury to the duodenum and small bowel. The colon has been mobilized, and the extraction process begins.

The surgeon dissects the mesentery of the right colon, identifying the small bowel below. The falciform ligament is visible at the top in the anatomic midline of the abdomen. The dissection continues across the transverse colon mesentery, avoiding the small bowel, and progresses towards the splenic flexure.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the splenic flexure, staying close to the colon to minimize injury risk. The stomach is visible on the left side, and the surgeon avoids it to prevent accidental damage. The dissection progresses towards the left side of the abdomen.

The surgeon uses a hand-assisted technique to mobilize the colon, removing the gel port and lifting the colon up and out. The terminal ileum is identified, and the anti-mesenteric fat is removed. The ileum is transected flush with the ileocecal valve, creating the staple line for the future efferent limb of the J pouch. The colon is essentially removed, and the fan and seal incision is visible post-surgery.

The surgeon identifies a black tattoo mark near the ileostomy in the right lower quadrant, which the patient requested to preserve. A cross marking for an unused stoma is visible on the opposite side. The ileostomy is being mobilized from the anterior abdominal wall as part of the second stage of a three-stage procedure, preparing for the removal of the rectum and creation of the ileal J pouch.

The surgeon uses a biofilm to reduce scar tissue formation while mobilizing the ileostomy. Care is taken to preserve blood vessels, with visible bleeding controlled. Adhesions in the right lower edge are lysed to ensure the small bowel can be repositioned into the pelvis for the ileal J-pouch construction.

The surgeon uses a stapler to close off the end of the ileostomy, which will become the end of the ileum or the efferent limb of the J-pouch. The healed incision is shown, marked off carefully for aesthetic purposes.

The surgeon carefully ensures that the incisions are straight, marking them with lines for precise approximation. The dissection proceeds through the abdominal wall, encountering scar tissue that makes the planes more challenging and occasionally bloody. The dissection continues down to the fascia and muscle, where a wound protector is eventually placed.

The surgeon opens the incision down to the muscle layer and places a plastic wound protector to reduce infection risk. The ileostomy is visible at the top left of the screen as the wound protector is positioned to shield the skin from bacteria.

The surgeon uses a wound protector to improve visualization of the pelvis. An instrument pulls the ileal J pouch down, assessing its length to ensure it can reach the anus. The pouch consists of two loops of intestine folded back on themselves.

The surgeon removes the rectum and creates the ileal J pouch, which is then connected to the anus. The rectal stump is visible after being stapled off. Incisions are made on either side, and the suprahemorrhoidal artery is identified and developed. The tissue ligator is used to control bleeding, allowing entry into the presacral space, also known as the total mesorectal plane.

The surgeon continues dissection in the mesorectal plane, revealing a fat plane with a small blood vessel crossing it. The dissection proceeds circumferentially around the rectum, mobilizing it down to the pelvic floor. The anatomical plane is bloodless, minimizing blood loss and reducing the risk of genitourinary dysfunction. The dissection avoids the prostate, as the patient is male, and prepares for rectum transection.

The surgeon performs mesenteric lengthening by making divisions within the mesentery while preserving the visible ilicolic artery running along the midline. The J pouch is folded back on itself, resembling the shape of a 'J', as shown in the schematic on the right.

The J pouch is visually folded back on itself and pulled down to ensure it reaches the pelvis. Marks in the mesentery are visible in the middle of the screen, used for additional lengthening. The two limbs of the J pouch are oriented and stitches are placed at the apex to prepare for anastomosis to the anus.

The surgeon points to the efferent limb of the J pouch, secured with a stitch to prevent twisting. The afferent limb is held up, showing potential inflammation. A small enterotomy is made at the base of the J pouch to gain access, and a large surgical stapler is inserted, causing minor tearing but not damaging the pouch.

The surgeon uses a 100mm stapler with two lines and a knife in between to fire and seal the J-pouch, fusing its limbs together. The staple line is visible in the middle of the J-pouch, which is being reinserted to extend its length. The completed J-pouch measures between fifteen to twenty-five centimeters and has a sponge at the bottom to keep it dry.

The surgeon demonstrates two methods for reconstructing after the J-pouch creation. The first method involves using a double stapling technique, where a stapler is placed across the rectum as low as possible, followed by another stapler from the bottom to secure the anastomosis with surgical staples. This technique may leave some rectal lining, which can potentially lead to inflammation.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn ileoanal anastomosis, removing the mucosa from the lowermost portion. A Lone Star Retractor is used to efface the area, with its sharp hooks visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a Lone Star Retractor to efface the anus, gaining access to the anal canal. A hand-sewn ileoanal anastomosis is performed, distinct from the double staple technique. The surgeon identifies the dentate line with a Bovie device, scoring it to mark the location for mucosectomy, aiming to remove all mucosal lining.

The surgeon performs a circumferential dissection by infiltrating saline to raise the mucosa from the underlying smooth muscle, identified as the internal anal sphincter. The mucosa is carefully separated without injuring the sphincter to preserve its function, and dissection continues around the area.

The surgeon uses forceps to pull down and remove the mucosa, carefully dissecting in the submucosal plane to avoid the underlying smooth muscle, which appears white. After completing the mucosectomy, the J pouch is pulled through the remaining cuff of muscle, leaving the cut edge of the mucosa visible with the intact muscle underneath.

The J pouch is brought down from the top view and positioned using sutures placed at four quadrant areas to secure it. The edges of the pouch are stitched onto the anal side, incorporating both muscle and mucosa. The posterior side of the J pouch is held by mesentery, highlighting the importance of length in reaching the anus.

The surgeon completes the hand-sewn ileoanal anastomosis, laying down the tissue and sewing it in place. A diverting loop ileostomy is created, with the incision visible and the ileostomy shown in its final form.
